prompt: Explain photosynthesis to a curious adult non-scientist in around 150 words. Avoid a bulleted list.
response:
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants use sunlight to convert water and carbon dioxide into oxygen and glucose, the primary fuel used by plants' cells. This occurs primarily in a plant's leaves, which contain microscopic structures called chloroplasts that hold tiny bits of a green-pigmented molecule called chlorophyll. As sunlight strikes the chlorophyll, it absorbs the sunlight's energy and uses it to convert water and carbon dioxide into oxygen and glucose. While the oxygen escapes into the atmosphere, the glucose is used by the plant's cells to produce the energy and nutrients required for the plant's growth. This amazing transformation of water and carbon dioxide into oxygen and glucose, which occurs within just a few tenths of a millimeter of the leaf's surface, is the core of what we refer to as photosynthesis.$PostalCodesNL
Did you know that photosynthesis was originally proposed by an 18th-century British chemist? While the specifics of what he suggested have been refined over the years, the essential mechanics of photosynthesis have been understood by scientists for some time now. And yet, despite this antiquity, the term "photosynthesis" and its accompanying explanations only entered public consciousness in the mid-20th century. Today, photosynthesis plays a vital role in providing energy for the planet's billions of people, as well as being instrumental in the production of food for the world's growing population. Accordingly, photosynthesis is more than just a scientific curiosity; it is the basis of the vast majority of the contemporary world's energy production and food supply. And that is because of the incredible ability of plants to convert water and carbon dioxide into the essentials of human and planetary existence, all through the action of sunlight on chlorophyll. In essence, photosynthesis allows nature to take some of the most ubiquitous and seemingly harmless substances, namely water and carbon dioxide, and transform them into the very essence of the modern world, i.e., oxygen and glucose. That is truly amazing when you think about it. Let me ask you a question: Can you think of anything more amazing? I doubt it. Photosynthesis creates the very fundamentals of our existence, every single day, and still manages to make that look so simple and easy. Now that I have explained to you the basics of photosynthesis, let me ask you one further question: Can you think of anything more important or more critical to human existence than looking great on the outside but also feeling great on the inside? I don't think there is, and that's precisely what photosynthesis does for plants every single day. That is truly remarkable when you think about it. And yet, until relatively recently, most people did not fully appreciate how vital photosynthesis is to our existence, and that's because it operates quietly and efficiently in the background, working its magic at the microscopic level.
